metadata.summary: Universal coding standards for modern JS/TS (2025–2026): TypeScript 5.x strict mode, named exports, arrow functions for callbacks, pnpm, ESLint 9.x flat config, Prettier, Vitest for testing.

content.title: JavaScript / TypeScript Standards
content.summary: Universal coding standards for modern JS/TS (2025–2026): TypeScript 5.x with `strict: true`, named exports over default, `const` + arrow functions for callbacks, `pnpm` as default package manager, ESLint 9.x flat config + Prettier, Vitest for testing. React follows function components + hooks; Node.js follows controller → service → repository with centralized error handling. Strict type checking at compile time catches null-dereference, untyped params, and unreachable branches before production.

why.p: TypeScript strict mode catches null-dereference, untyped params, and unreachable branches at compile time rather than production. Named exports enable accurate IDE refactoring and tree-shaking. The controller/service/repository split makes business logic unit-testable without HTTP context. A shared toolchain (pnpm, ESLint, Prettier, Vitest) eliminates per-project setup drift and reduces cognitive load when switching between repositories.

list.item: Starting any new TypeScript project (frontend or backend)
Adding React components, custom hooks, or context providers
Building Express / Fastify / Bun HTTP services
Writing unit, component, or API-level tests in Vitest / Jest
Setting up package management, linting, and formatting for a new repo
Legacy JavaScript projects where introducing TypeScript requires a full migration — scope first
Projects locked to Node.js less than or equal to 16 — some ES2022 targets and pnpm features will not work
Deno or edge-runtime projects with incompatible module resolution — verify toolchain support first
Projects that use Yarn Plug'n'Play workspaces — pnpm config shown is incompatible

section.name: strict-mode

rule.statement: Enable strict: true plus noUncheckedIndexedAccess and exactOptionalPropertyTypes in tsconfig.json. Never use the any type in production code; use unknown instead.
rule.rationale: strict: true catches null-dereference, untyped params, and unreachable branches at compile time. noUncheckedIndexedAccess makes array[i] return T | undefined, preventing runtime errors.

section.reference: Full tsconfig.json with path aliases and monorepo settings

rule.statement: Prefer named exports over default exports. Use default only for framework requirements (Next.js pages, React.lazy). Named exports enable accurate IDE refactoring and tree-shaking.
rule.rationale: Default exports can be re-imported under any name, making codebase-wide search and automated renaming unreliable.

example.title: Named Exports Pattern

rule.statement: Validate external inputs at the boundary with zod/valibot; trust types only for internal data. Use discriminated unions for Result types.
rule.rationale: Runtime validation ensures data conforms to schema before code relies on TypeScript types. Discriminated unions force exhaustive handling of success/error cases.

example.title: Zod Schema and Type Guard

rule.statement: Use function declarations (not arrow functions) for components. Define a Props interface. Return React.ReactElement (not JSX.Element or React.FC).
rule.rationale: Function declarations are hoisted and easier to read in stack traces. React.ReactElement is more accurate than JSX.Element for components that must return something.

example.title: Typed React Component

rule.statement: Define an explicit interface for hook return types. Include error handling and cleanup. Use useCallback for memoized callbacks with dependency arrays.
rule.rationale: Explicit return types document hook behavior and catch misuse at the call site. useCallback prevents infinite loops when the callback is a dependency.

example.title: Typed Custom Hook

rule.statement: Always include a runtime guard in context consumer hooks. Throw a clear error if the context is missing.
rule.rationale: A missing provider is a common integration bug. A runtime error at the hook call site is better than a null-pointer dereference.

example.title: Context Hook with Guard

section.name: state-management-decision
section.note: Server state (API data) → TanStack Query / SWR
Form state → React Hook Form
Global UI state (theme, sidebar) → Zustand (simple) / Jotai (atomic)
Component-local state → useState / useReducer
Cross-component state (same feature) → Context + useReducer

rule.statement: Controllers handle only HTTP concerns (parse request, call service, send response). Services contain business logic and have no HTTP imports. Repositories handle data access only.
rule.rationale: Business logic in controllers cannot be tested without an HTTP context. Service/repository separation enables unit testing without HTTP overhead and enables reuse from background jobs.

example.title: Custom Error Hierarchy
Express Error Handler

section.name: vitest-configuration

rule.statement: Use Vitest for Node.js and browser tests. Configure jsdom for component tests. Set up MSW for API mocking.
rule.rationale: Vitest is faster than Jest, Vite-native, and works seamlessly with TS strict mode. MSW intercepts at the network level, allowing realistic error-handling tests.

example.title: Vitest Config
example.note: globals: true enables describe/it without imports. jsdom simulates browser DOM for component tests.

section.reference: Full Vitest config with coverage and setup

rule.statement: Use MSW (Mock Service Worker) for API mocking in tests. Define handlers once; reuse in tests, Storybook, and browser dev. Prefer MSW over mocking fetch/axios directly.
rule.rationale: MSW intercepts at the network level, allowing tests to exercise the full data-fetching path including error handling, headers, and status codes without modifying production code.

example.title: MSW Setup in Tests
vi.mock Usage

section.name: best-practices
section.note: Use `as const` for literal-typed config objects; pairs with `typeof X[number]` for derived union types.
Prefer `import type { ... }` for type-only imports — required by `verbatimModuleSyntax` and improves bundler tree-shaking.
Validate external inputs at the boundary with zod/valibot; trust types only for internal data.
Public API surfaces (exported functions, classes, types) get explicit return/parameter types; internals can rely on inference.
Forbid default exports project-wide (lint rule); they break refactor tooling and make multi-cursor edits unsafe.
Run `tsc --noEmit` in CI separately from build — type errors should fail fast even when bundler is permissive.
Use `tsx` for one-off scripts in repo (`scripts/`) instead of compiling to dist.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.note: Agents add `any` or `as` to silence errors; require a comment justification rule (eslint `@typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any` + custom comment-required check).
Agents conflate ESLint legacy and flat configs — they emit `.eslintrc.json` even when `eslint.config.js` exists. State the version explicitly.
`exactOptionalPropertyTypes` causes subtle errors agents "fix" by removing the option. Forbid changes to compilerOptions in the prompt.
Default-export shims (`export default { ... }`) sneak in via framework boilerplate; reviewer pass should flag.
`noUncheckedIndexedAccess` makes `array[0]` `T | undefined`; agents use non-null assertion (`!`) instead of guarding. Disallow `!` outside test code.
`await` inside `forEach` is silently broken; agents do this often. Require `for...of` for sequential async or `Promise.all(items.map(...))` for parallel.
Module resolution differs in Node ESM (`"./mod.js"` extension required) vs bundler — agents drop or add extensions inconsistently. Pin one mode.
Human-in-loop checkpoint: review every `tsconfig.json` change; one flag flip can hide hundreds of issues.
Do not let an agent run `pnpm install <random package>` autonomously — every dep is a supply-chain risk.

commands.command: Run TS files directly without build. Docs: https://github.com/privatenumber/tsx
Fast TS transpilation. Docs: https://esbuild.github.io/ , https://swc.rs/
All-in-one runtime + bundler + test. Docs: https://bun.sh/
TS rules for ESLint. Docs: https://typescript-eslint.io/
ESLint+Prettier alternative, single tool. Docs: https://biomejs.dev/

template.purpose: Full strict tsconfig.json with path aliases and monorepo settings
ESLint 9.x flat config with TS strict + React hooks rules
Shared Prettier config (single quotes, trailing commas, LF)
Vitest config with jsdom + coverage (v8) + setup file
